Course Title: Vendor AI Claims: Separating Reality from Marketing
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with everyday business software and a general awareness of how AI tools are being discussed in the workplace is all you need to get started with this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify common AI marketing claims and explain the key questions to ask when evaluating whether a vendor's technology delivers genuine value. You will also apply a practical framework to assess "AI-powered" products critically, helping you make more informed purchasing and procurement decisions for your organisation.

lessons.lesson title: Vendor AI Claims: Separating Reality from Marketing
lessons.Description: In today's marketplace, the term "AI-powered" appears on almost every new product and service, making it increasingly difficult to know what vendors actually mean when they use it. This lesson will help you cut through the noise by introducing practical frameworks for evaluating AI claims with confidence, even if you have no technical background. You will learn to ask the right questions, spot common marketing tactics, and identify the warning signs that suggest a claim may be exaggerated or misleading. By the end of the lesson, you will feel better equipped to assess whether a vendor's AI offering genuinely meets your needs or simply sounds impressive on paper.

slides.subtitle: Cutting through the AI marketing noise
slides.slidecontent:
• AI is everywhere in vendor marketing right now
• Bold claims often outpace what the technology actually delivers
• Knowing what questions to ask protects your budget and your team
• Not all "AI-powered" labels mean the same thing
• This course gives you practical tools to evaluate what vendors are really offering

slides.subtitle: What vendors actually mean by their buzzwords
slides.slidecontent:
• "AI-powered" often means basic automation or keyword matching
• Vendors rarely specify which type of AI they actually use
• Terms like "intelligent" and "smart" are marketing language, not technical descriptions
• No industry standard defines what counts as AI in a product
• The boldest claims tend to appear where evidence is thinnest

slides.subtitle: Why vague language dominates vendor pitches
slides.slidecontent:
• Marketing teams drive the wording, not technical teams
• Buzzwords replace precise technical explanations
• Vague claims invite customers to fill gaps with assumptions
• "Next-generation" and similar phrases sound impressive but mean nothing
• Lack of standard definitions allows anyone to use AI labels freely

slides.subtitle: Independent testing and reliable benchmarks
slides.slidecontent:
• Run standardised benchmark tests for consistent comparison
• Check independent audit results from unbiased third parties
• Look for reproducible performance data, not just vendor figures
• Seek credible reviews from researchers or industry analysts
• Compare vendor claims directly against available evidence

slides.subtitle: What to ask during sales conversations
slides.slidecontent:
• Request live demos with real data, not polished videos
• Ask for specific accuracy figures and how they were measured
• Find out if the AI was trained on data relevant to your industry
• Clarify who is responsible when the system makes errors
• Determine whether human review is still required at any stage

slides.slidetitle: Major Red Flags in AI Claims
slides.subtitle: Warning signs that demand immediate scrutiny
slides.slidecontent:
• Claims of "100% accuracy" or "fully autonomous" operation
• No disclosed error rates or system limitations
• Inability to explain what the AI actually does
• Zero real-world examples or independent testing
• Pressure to decide quickly without proper evaluation
• No clear accountability when errors occur

slides.subtitle: Common patterns in oversold AI products
slides.slidecontent:
• Cherry-picked case studies showing only best outcomes
• Guaranteed results promised despite AI's inherent uncertainty
• Evasive answers when asked about failure scenarios
• Heavy reliance on impressive-sounding but meaningless terminology
• Reluctance to provide technical details or testing methodology
• Claims that seem too good to be true for the price point

slides.subtitle: Core principles for smart AI procurement
slides.slidecontent:
• Always demand real evidence, not marketing materials
• Test systems in your own environment before committing
• Treat vague claims as red flags requiring deeper investigation
• Push for specific, measurable results with clear methodology
• Maintain healthy skepticism throughout the evaluation process

scripts.script: Welcome, and thanks for joining this course. If you've spent any time looking at software or technology solutions recently, you'll have noticed that "AI" seems to appear in just about every vendor's marketing materials. It's on the brochures, the websites, the sales decks. But here's the thing: bold claims and actual capability are not always the same thing, and that gap can be costly if you don't know what to look for. Not every product labelled "AI-powered" is doing something genuinely intelligent, and some are doing far less than the marketing suggests. This course is designed to help you cut through that noise. We'll give you practical, straightforward tools to ask the right questions, spot the red flags, and make more confident decisions when vendors come to you with their latest pitch.

Let's start by pulling back the curtain on what vendors actually mean when they use AI terminology. When a product is described as "AI-powered," that phrase can mean almost anything. In many cases, it simply refers to basic automated rules or keyword matching, nothing remotely sophisticated. Vendors rarely tell you which type of AI they're actually using, because the terminology is vague enough that they don't have to. Words like "intelligent" and "smart" are marketing choices, not technical descriptions. There's no agreed industry standard that defines what qualifies as AI in a product, so anyone can use the label freely. What you'll often find is that the loudest, boldest claims appear precisely where the actual evidence is weakest.

Understanding how these claims get crafted helps explain why they're so often misleading. The wording is driven almost entirely by marketing teams rather than technical ones. The goal is to sound impressive and modern, not to be precise. Instead of explaining what the technology actually does, vendors reach for buzzwords that do heavy lifting without saying much. This creates a dangerous dynamic: vague language leaves gaps, and we naturally fill those gaps with our own assumptions. We hear "AI-powered" and imagine something far more sophisticated than what might actually be running behind the scenes. This is exactly the pattern we need to recognise and guard against.

So how do we actually put vendor claims to the test? The most reliable approach starts with standardised benchmarks. These are structured tests that measure AI performance consistently, so you can compare systems on a level playing field. But benchmarks alone aren't enough. Look for independent audits carried out by organisations with no financial interest in making a product look good. Can the performance results be reproduced by third parties? Are there credible reviews from researchers or industry analysts who have examined the system independently? If impressive figures only appear in vendor marketing materials and nobody else can replicate them, that's a significant warning sign.

When you're in conversation with vendors, here are practical ways to cut through polished pitches. First, ask for live demonstrations using real data, not pre-recorded videos where everything goes perfectly. Push them on accuracy claims. Numbers like "95% accurate" mean nothing without knowing how that figure was measured and under what conditions. Ask whether the AI was trained on data from your industry, because systems built for one sector often behave differently in another. And here's a question that catches many vendors off guard: who is accountable when the system makes a mistake? These questions reveal far more than any sales presentation.

Now let's look at the warning signs that should make you slow down and ask harder questions. Be immediately sceptical of any claim that a system is "100% accurate" or "fully autonomous" because no AI system is, period. If vendors can't tell you error rates or system limitations, that's a problem. Watch for inability to explain what their AI actually does beyond vague terminology. If they can't point to real-world examples or independent testing, you're essentially taking their word for everything. And be very cautious about guaranteed results, because AI operates in complex, unpredictable environments where responsible vendors don't make such promises.

Beyond obvious red flags, watch for subtler patterns in how AI products are oversold. Case studies that show only cherry-picked outcomes are a common tactic. Be wary of guaranteed results despite AI's inherent uncertainty, and pay attention to evasive answers when you ask about failure scenarios. Many vendors rely heavily on terminology that sounds impressive but carries no real meaning. If they're reluctant to provide technical details or explain their testing methodology, that reluctance itself tells you something important. And if claims seem too good to be true for the price point, they probably are.

Let's wrap up with a framework you can apply to any AI evaluation. Always demand real evidence, not just marketing materials or vendor-created case studies. Test systems in your own environment before making commitments, because what works for one organisation may not work for yours. Treat vague claims as red flags that require deeper investigation. Push consistently for specific, measurable results with clear methodology behind them. And throughout the process, maintain healthy skepticism. Being skeptical doesn't mean being negative, it means being smart. This approach will serve you well every time someone promises their AI will transform your business.

Who is this for: Procurement managers, purchasing officers, business analysts, project managers, department heads, IT managers, operations managers, finance managers, executives making technology decisions
Team or Dept this is for: Procurement, IT, Operations, Finance, Legal, Executive Leadership
